impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products allegedly lack substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers agreeing to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors facing exclusionary tying arrangements by patent holders now have strong precedent for antitrust challenges where the tied product has uses beyond the scope of the tying patent.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle licenses with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust liability risk. Companies must carefully evaluate whether their unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses before implementing such arrangements.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing emulated function calls to a model are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 and Alice step one, and whether damages may be awarded for foreign sales of infringing software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's sales to customers outside the United States. This decision significantly impacts patent eligibility for software security patents and limits extraterritorial patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing cybersecurity patent assertions benefit from strengthened § 101 eligibility defenses and a clear limitation on damages based on foreign sales of software products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software fields face heightened invalidity risk under § 101 and cannot recover damages based on foreign product sales, significantly reducing potential recovery in patent litigation.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims where the patent applicant allegedly withheld prior art documents it acquired through a corporate acquisition.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment of inequitable conduct against Tenaris and summary judgment in Tenaris's favor on Global Tubing's Walker Process fraud claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves allegations that Tenaris withheld prior art CYMAX documents from the PTO when prosecuting patents on its BlueCoil coiled tubing product. The decision sends both the patent enforceability and antitrust claims back for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to allow Walker Process antitrust fraud claims to proceed to trial, providing leverage against patents obtained through alleged fraud on the PTO.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that acquire assets including prior art documents face heightened risk that failure to disclose such documents during patent prosecution may support both inequitable conduct and antitrust fraud claims.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims covering a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a jury verdict of infringement and held that GoTV's streaming media patents claiming server-side tailoring of content to device capabilities were ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device characteristics. This ruling required entry of judgment for Netflix, eliminating a $2.5 million damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain additional protection against patent assertions covering routine content-delivery optimization techniques, reinforcing § 101 as a defense against abstract software method patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content adaptation or optimization face increased invalidity risk under § 101, limiting monetization of such patent portfolios.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the deadline for a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) in the original district court action may obtain a mandatory stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling a new declaratory judgment action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay of a district court patent action under § 1659(a)(2) by dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action. The decision prevents gamesmanship with the ITC stay provision and ensures that pharmaceutical companies cannot manipulate parallel proceedings to delay resolution of patent disputes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies seeking expeditious resolution of patent disputes in district court are protected from opponents' tactical refiling of actions to obtain mandatory stays they previously failed to timely request.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day deadline for requesting a mandatory stay under § 1659(a) cannot cure the deficiency by refiling their district court action, limiting strategic options for managing parallel ITC and district court proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's internal instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing PTAB discretionary denials of IPR institution are general policy statements exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution decisions are insulated from judicial review. This preserves the PTO's flexibility in managing the IPR system.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained flexibility to deny IPR institution, as the Director's discretionary framework for declining petitions remains intact without the constraints of formal rulemaking.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that rely on IPRs to challenge patents face continued uncertainty about discretionary denials, with no ability to challenge the PTO's institution framework through APA rulemaking process challenges.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watch models infringing Masimo's blood oxygen measurement patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This decision has major commercial implications for Apple's flagship wearable product line.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with strong portfolios in wearable health technology can effectively use ITC proceedings to obtain exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating patented health-sensing features into consumer electronics face significant import ban risk through ITC proceedings, potentially requiring product redesigns or licensing arrangements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the challenged claims were obvious over the prior art under the correct construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR of DivX's streaming media encryption patent, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision is significant for the streaming media industry as it reopens the validity challenge to DivX's patent covering systems for streaming partially encrypted content.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to correct PTAB claim construction errors, preserving the ability to challenge patents through IPR proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the media streaming and encryption space face continued validity challenges when the Federal Circuit disagrees with Board claim constructions that favored patentability.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent case against Microsoft involving Azure cloud networking technology, and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The decision reinforces that district courts must not impose unduly stringent requirements on damages experts at the Daubert stage in patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the Federal Circuit's correction of overly aggressive Daubert exclusions of damages evidence, preserving their ability to present damages cases to juries.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft face reduced ability to obtain summary judgment through Daubert challenges to damages experts, as the Federal Circuit signals skepticism of exclusions that effectively eliminate a plaintiff's case.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act when the SBA failed to award grants in the order applications were received.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants could pursue Tucker Act claims against the United States for unpaid RRF grants. The court held that 15 U.S.C. § 9009c's mandatory language ('shall award grants... in the order in which applications are received') created an enforceable obligation, giving applicants standing to seek damages when the SBA disbursed funds out of order. This opens the door to significant potential government liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that applied for but were denied federal pandemic relief grants in violation of statutory priority rules can pursue damages claims against the government under the Tucker Act.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability to hundreds of businesses for failure to administer pandemic relief funds in the statutorily mandated order, which could set precedent for claims arising from other mandatory grant programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses beyond PACER's operation.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by using PACER fees to fund unrelated court technology expenses. The court upheld the settlement, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives, finding that hundreds of thousands of PACER users would be fully or substantially reimbursed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that paid PACER fees during the eight-year class period will receive reimbursement. The decision validates the class action mechanism for challenging excessive government fees.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a means-plus-function claim term in a crop harvester patent was indefinite where the specification disclosed corresponding structure using terms like 'conventional in design and operation' and described the structure at a general level.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that claims of Gramm's harvester sensor patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification adequately disclosed corresponding structure for the means-plus-function 'control means' limitation. The decision is significant for agricultural technology companies and clarifies the standard for indefiniteness of means-plus-function claims when the specification describes structures at a general or conventional level.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from the Federal Circuit's more permissive approach to finding adequate structural disclosure, particularly where the specification references 'conventional' structures known in the art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Deere face reduced ability to invalidate patents on indefiniteness grounds when the specification provides at least a general description of the corresponding structure for means-plus-function terms.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with the governing statute. The court affirmed the CIT's ruling that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings and that the Commission must evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies and the public gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire responses from disclosure regardless of whether they contain genuinely confidential information.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting business data in ITC antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk that their commercially sensitive information may be disclosed, as the ITC must now evaluate confidentiality claims individually rather than applying blanket protection.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Indian Health Service registered nurses hired under Title 5 but paid under Title 38's special salary provisions are entitled to enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed dismissal of a class action by IHS registered nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday work during the COVID-19 pandemic. The court held that nurses paid at Title 38 base rates were entitled to pursue claims for enhanced compensation under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and related regulations. This decision potentially affects all similarly situated current and former IHS nurses.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces significant potential backpay liability for IHS nurses who worked overtime and non-standard hours, particularly during the pandemic, and this precedent may extend to other Title 5 healthcare employees receiving Title 38 pay enhancements.
